content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
, 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) ;J:Z:.? -5 660 • F:1x (916) ;J:.? :.? -0886 
-
Jennifer M. Lyon 
General Counsel, SDCCC 
McDougal Love Boehmer 
Foley Lyon & Canlas 
La Mesa Village Plaza 
8100 La Mesa Blvd., Suite 200 
La Mesa, CA 91942 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-022 
Dear Ms. Lyon: 
March 29, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act {the "Act")1 and Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we do 
not advise on any other area oflaw, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of 
interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c){3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section I 097.1 (c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit San Diego Convention Center Corporation, Inc. ("SDCCC") 
from amending, extending, modifying, supplementing by administrative policy, or re-negotiating 
the contract with Centerplate during Director Lai's tenure on the Board? 
CONCLUSION 
Section 1090 does not prohibit SDCCC from negotiating or modifying the contract with 
Centerplate because the remote interest exception of Section 1091 {b )(17) applies. Director Lai must 
recuse himself from any decisions involving contracts with Centerplate and refrain from providing 
any advice to SDCCC regarding any such contracts. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-022 
Page No. 2 
The San Diego Convention Center Corporation, Inc. is a California nonprofit public benefit 
corporation. As reflected in its Amended and Restated Bylaws ("Bylaws"), SDCCC was fonned for 
public benefit purposes to operate and manage the publicly owned San Diego Convention Center 
("Convention Center"), including its financing, expansion and maintenance. 
SDCCC operates as a special purpose governmental unit and a component to the City of San 
Diego's tourism, economic development and revenue programs. The City of San Diego is the sole 
member of SDCCC. The City acts through the City Council pursuant to the City's Charter. SDCCC 
has a seven-member appointed Board of Directors. The Mayor appoints one director as a 
representative of the Mayor, and the City Council appoints one director as a representative of City 
Council. The remaining directors are appointed by the Mayor with City Council confirmation. 
SDCCC hires a variety of contractors to provide services in connection with events at the 
Convention Center. Effective October 1, 2007, SDCCC engaged Service America Corporation, 
doing business as Centerplate ("Centerplate") to provide exclusive food and beverage services to 
the Convention Center pursuant to a Food and Beverage Services Contract ("Contract"). The initial 
nine-year and eight-month tenn was extended for an additional five-year period, and the extended 
Contract term will terminate on June 30, 2021. The Contract allows for administrative memoranda 
to supplement the agreement. 
Since 2007, Centerplate has been providing exclusive food, beverages (including alcoholic 
beverages) and other services related to providing food and beverages at the Convention Center. 
Under the Contract, Centerplate pays commissions to SDCCC based on a percentage of the gross 
sales revenue generated from Centerplate's services. Those commissions include the payment from 
Centerplate to SDCCC of a commission on gross sales revenue generated from alcoholic beverage 
services. The selection of food and beverage vendors is a matter of contract between Centerplate 
and SDCCC's licensees/clients who utilize designated areas within the Convention Center for a 
particular event or purpose. SDCCC does not select Centerplate's food and beverage vendors. 
In 2017, Centerplate started using Abnonnal Company, Inc. {"Abnonnal") as one of its 
alcoholic beverage vendors for client/licensee events at the Convention Center. Abnonnal 
manufactures beer, wine, and food through: Abnormal Beer Co., Abnonnal Wine Co. and The Cork 
and Craft@Abnonnal (a restaurant). Abnormal was incorporated on August 21, 2013 by its co-
founder Elvin Lai. Abnormal has a three-member board of directors. Mr. Lai is Abnormal 's Chief 
Financial Officer and owns shares in Abnormal. On December 5, 2017, the Council confirmed the 
Mayor's appointment of Mr. Lai to serve on SDCCC's Board of Directors. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Generally, Section 1090 prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Under this section, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig 


File No. A-18-022 
Page No. 3 
{1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, and the prohibition 
applies regardless of whether the tenns of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson 
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646~649.) Generally, when Section 1090 applies to one member of a 
governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board 
member abstain from the decision. The entire governing body is precluded from entering into the 
contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 647-649; Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569; 86 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 (2003). 
Section 1091 and 1091.5 establish exceptions to Section 1090 for a financial interest in a 
contract that is "remote interest" or "noninterest." If an official's interest is a "remote interest," an 
agency may execute a contract if (1) the officer in question discloses his or her financial interest in 
the contract to the agency, (2) the interest is noted in the agency's official records, and (3) the 
officer abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. (Section 1091(a).) If the 
official's interest is a "noninterest, an agency may execute the contract and the official is not 
required to abstain from the decision. Except in limited circumstances, a noninterest does not 
require any disclosure. (City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 
508, 514-515.) 
In this instance, it is not contested that Director Lai is a public officer subject to the 
provisions of Section 1090 and that any agreement between SDCCC and Centerplate to provide 
exclusive food and beverage services to the Convention Center is a contract for purposes of Section 
1090. Additionally, as a member of SDCCC's Board of Directors, Director Lai is presumed to be 
involved in the making of all contracts by SDCCC irrespective of whether he actually participates 
in the making of the contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 645, 649.) It is established that an official, 
who is also an officer, shareholder, and employee of a listed subcontractor, would clearly be 
"financially interested" in the contract since the contract would result in earnings for the company 
of which the official is an officer and shareholder, as well as in the official's own salary as an 
employee of the company. (See Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 645-646.) 
In this instance, the only issue implicated by your request for advice is whether Director 
Lai's financial interest in Abnonnal, as a subcontractor ofCenterplate, qualifies for an exception 
under Section 1090 as a "remote interest" in decisions regarding a contract between SDCCC and 
Centerplate. 
The remote interest under Section 1091(b)(l 7) is defined to includ~: 
"That of an owner or partner of a finn serving as an appointed member of an 
unelected board or commission of the contracting agency if the owner or partner 
recuses himself or herself from providing any advice to the contracting agency 
regarding the contract between the finn and the contracting agency and from all 
participation in reviewing a project that results from that contract." 
In this case, Director Lai is an appointed member of an unelected board and is the owner or 
partner of a finn. Accordingly, the remote exception applies and the Authority may amend its 
existing contract or enter into new contracts with Centerplate so long as Director Lai discloses his 
interest in Abnonnal, as a subcontractor of Centerplate, to the public agency, the interest is noted in 


File No. A-18-022 
Page No. 4 
the agency's official records, and Director Lai abstains from any participation in the making or 
approval of the contract. 
Under this Section 1091 (b )(17) remote interest, Director Lai is subject to additional 
requirements. Director Lai must recuse himself from providing any advice to SDCCC regarding any 
contract with Centerplate. Section 109l(c) states that the remote interest exception is not available 
if Director Lai influences or attempts to influence another member of SDCCC on this matter. 
We also note that, because the remedy in this situation is for him to abstain from any 
participation in the approval of such contracts (see Section 1091(a)), we do not analyze the conflict 
of interest under the Act as Director Lai's abstention under Section 1091(a) would also satisfy the 
requirements of the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
~.rr'Jk--
~
hary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
